summary: Dental caries (cavities) are treated based on severity. Mild cases respond to fluoride remineralization therapy. Moderate cavities require composite or amalgam restorations with 90-95% 10-year survival rates. Severe cases involving significant tooth structure loss need full-coverage crowns. Early detection and intervention significantly improve outcomes and reduce treatment complexity.
risk_factors: Poor oral hygiene and high sugar diet
History of previous restorations on the same tooth
Proximity of caries to pulp chamber
alternatives: Glass ionomer restoration (releases fluoride, good for root caries)
Amalgam filling (more durable for posterior teeth under heavy occlusal load)
Indirect ceramic inlay/onlay (for large cavities preserving more tooth structure)
Silver diamine fluoride (non-invasive arrest for elderly or pediatric patients)
referral_summary: Standard restorative procedure performed by general dentists. Referral to prosthodontist recommended for complex multi-surface restorations or when significant occlusal rehabilitation is needed.
patient_education: A cavity is a hole in your tooth caused by bacteria and acid. The dentist removes the decayed area and fills it with a tooth-colored material. After treatment, maintain good brushing habits (twice daily with fluoride toothpaste) and floss daily. Limit sugary snacks and drinks. Regular 6-month checkups help catch new cavities early when they're easier to treat.
